topicArea: Linguistics, Prescriptivism & the Authority of Standard Usage

questions.passage:
When the compilers of the great eighteenth-century dictionaries set out to fix the English language, as they frequently put it, they were responding to a specific anxiety rather than to abstract curiosity. Educated opinion of the period worried that English, unlike French, which possessed an official academy charged with policing usage, was drifting toward instability, and that a nation without a stable language risked a corresponding instability in its laws, its learning, and its sense of itself. A dictionary, on this view, was not merely a record of words already in use; it was an instrument for arresting a language's natural tendency to change, freezing it into a form precise and permanent enough to bear the weight of a civilization. The ambition was, in retrospect, almost certainly impossible from the outset, since no language has ever actually held still for any dictionary, however authoritative. That the ambition nonetheless succeeded in shaping educated attitudes toward correctness for the better part of three centuries is a separate matter from whether the ambition itself was ever coherent.

The view inherited from this project, generally called prescriptivism, holds that correct and incorrect linguistic forms exist independently of how people happen to speak, and that suitably qualified authorities, grammarians, dictionaries, style guides, are positioned to identify these forms and to correct departures from them. The rationale offered for this view typically appeals to clarity, continuity with a shared literary tradition, and the practical value of a stable, widely intelligible standard across a large and linguistically diverse population.

Modern linguistics has mounted a sustained challenge to this picture. Descriptive linguists observe that claims of correctness, examined historically, tend to codify the usage of whichever social group held the greatest educational and economic authority at a given moment, elevating that group's habits to the status of natural or logical necessity while treating the equally systematic, equally rule-governed usage of other groups as mere error or corruption. Every living language, on this account, has always been changing, continuously and without exception, so a narrative that treats change as decline mistakes a single historical snapshot, the usage current when a particular grammarian happened to be writing, for a timeless standard that never actually existed.

This descriptivist critique carries real force, but it does not, on its own, dissolve the practical difficulties prescriptivism was invoked to address. Linguists who study language variation are generally careful to acknowledge that unregulated variation carries genuine costs of its own: legal and technical communication depends on shared precision that a wholly unstandardized language could not reliably supply, and a great many social and economic institutions, whatever the underlying linguistic merits of the matter, grant access on the basis of command over a particular prestige register. Simply refusing, as a matter of principle, to discuss or teach any standard does nothing to dismantle this gatekeeping; it merely leaves the gate unremarked while it continues to function exactly as before, disadvantaging most severely the speakers who were never taught the register the gate happens to require.

A further complication cuts against descriptivism's claim to occupy neutral ground. Compiling even a strictly descriptive dictionary or grammar requires choices that are not themselves neutral: whose speech gets sampled, which registers are included as representative, which forms are recorded as established usage and which are dismissed as slang, error, or dialect unworthy of documentation. These choices relocate, rather than eliminate, the normative judgment that descriptivism claims to have abandoned. A dictionary that quietly excludes the usage of a marginalized community from its corpus, on the grounds that this usage is not yet sufficiently established, has made exactly the kind of gatekeeping decision prescriptivism makes explicitly, only without acknowledging that a decision has been made at all.

Some linguists and language educators have proposed a working compromise in light of this difficulty: teach the prestige standard openly as one register among several, useful for navigating specific institutional contexts such as formal writing or professional communication, without asserting that this register is inherently superior to, more logical than, or more correct than any other dialect a student already speaks fluently and correctly by the standards of its own community. This decouples the practical usefulness of standard usage from any claim about its inherent linguistic superiority. The compromise remains genuinely contested, however, since it can be read in two very different ways: as a respectful, non-judgmental acknowledgment of linguistic diversity, or as a more diplomatically worded version of the same demand, that speakers of nonstandard varieties learn to produce the prestige register on command, dressed in language less openly dismissive of their existing speech than older prescriptivist rhetoric tended to be.

None of this can be resolved simply by adopting the correct theoretical stance. The practical stakes of standard usage, who gets believed in a courtroom, hired after an interview, published by an editor, persist regardless of what linguistic science establishes about the underlying equality of dialects as internally coherent, fully rule-governed systems. The most honest position available may be an uncomfortable one: to affirm, as linguistics has convincingly shown, that no dialect is inherently more logical or correct than any other, while acknowledging, without thereby endorsing as fair, that one register nonetheless carries disproportionate social power that a purely descriptive science, however rigorous, has no internal resources to neutralize on its own.
questions.stem: Which of the following best expresses the main idea of the passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Because all dialects are equally logical and rule-governed, teaching any prestige standard in schools is indefensible and should be abandoned entirely as a pedagogical goal.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Descriptive linguistics shows no dialect is inherently more correct, yet the prestige standard's social power remains, unaddressed by rule-giving or neutrality alone.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Correct linguistic usage exists independently of social context and should be identified and enforced by qualified grammatical authorities.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Descriptive dictionaries are compiled more objectively than prescriptive ones, since they simply record whatever usage already exists in a language.

explanations.A: This choice draws a policy conclusion far beyond what the passage supports. The author discusses a pedagogical compromise of teaching a prestige standard alongside acknowledgment of other dialects' equal validity, without ever concluding that teaching any standard is indefensible; the final paragraph explicitly notes that a prestige register carries disproportionate social power regardless of linguistic theory, which argues against simply abandoning instruction in it.
explanations.B: Correct. The passage's overall argument holds two claims together: that descriptive linguistics has convincingly shown no dialect is inherently more correct than another, and that this finding does not by itself dissolve the real social and institutional power a prestige standard continues to hold, a tension neither prescriptivism nor descriptivism alone fully resolves.
explanations.C: This choice restates the prescriptivist position the passage subjects to sustained critique, without the qualifications the author applies to it. The third paragraph directly challenges the claim that correctness exists independently of social context, arguing that such claims typically codify one social group's usage as natural while stigmatizing others.
explanations.D: This choice is directly undermined by the fifth paragraph, which argues that even descriptive dictionaries require normative choices about which usage to sample and document, meaning descriptive compilation is not simply a matter of neutrally recording whatever usage already exists.

questions.stem: The primary function of the fifth paragraph is to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: argue that descriptivism cannot claim full neutrality, since even a descriptive record requires normative choices.

choices.label: B
choices.text: prove that descriptive dictionaries are less accurate than prescriptive dictionaries at documenting a language's actual vocabulary.

choices.label: C
choices.text: demonstrate that prescriptivist grammarians were more honest about their normative judgments than descriptive linguists have been.

choices.label: D
choices.text: introduce the pedagogical compromise discussed later in the passage as the solution that finally dissolves the problem of linguistic gatekeeping.

explanations.A: Correct. The fifth paragraph argues that compiling even a descriptive record of language requires choices, whose speech is sampled, which registers are included, that are not themselves neutral, showing that descriptivism relocates rather than eliminates the kind of normative judgment it criticizes in prescriptivism.
explanations.B: This choice attributes to the paragraph a comparative claim about accuracy that it does not make. The fifth paragraph does not compare the accuracy of descriptive and prescriptive dictionaries at documenting vocabulary; it argues that descriptive compilation involves its own hidden normative choices, a different point entirely.
explanations.C: This choice reverses the paragraph's actual claim. Rather than praising prescriptivist grammarians for greater honesty, the paragraph argues that prescriptivism makes its normative choices explicitly while descriptivism makes similar choices implicitly, without necessarily framing either group's honesty as superior to the other's.
explanations.D: This choice misplaces the pedagogical compromise, which the passage introduces in the sixth paragraph, not the fifth. The fifth paragraph's function is to complicate descriptivism's claim to neutrality, a point made before the compromise is discussed as a possible practical response.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward the compromise of teaching a prestige standard as one useful register among several, described in the sixth paragraph, is best characterized as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: fully approving, since the author presents this compromise as a definitive resolution to the long-running tension between prescriptivism and descriptivism.

choices.label: B
choices.text: dismissive, since the author regards the compromise as no different in substance from traditional prescriptivist rhetoric.

choices.label: C
choices.text: measured, acknowledging its potential value while noting genuine disagreement over whether it treats nonstandard speakers fairly.

choices.label: D
choices.text: hostile, since the author believes teaching any prestige standard necessarily disrespects students' home dialects.

explanations.A: This choice overstates the author's endorsement. The sixth paragraph explicitly states that the compromise remains genuinely contested and can be read in two very different ways, which is inconsistent with treating it as a definitive resolution to the underlying tension.
explanations.B: This choice understates the author's engagement with the compromise's potential value. While the passage does note that the compromise can be read as diplomatically worded prescriptivism, it also presents this as only one of two legitimate readings, not as the author's settled dismissal of the entire proposal.
explanations.C: Correct. The sixth paragraph presents the compromise as potentially valuable, decoupling practical usefulness from claims of inherent superiority, while explicitly acknowledging that it remains genuinely contested and can be interpreted either as respectful acknowledgment of diversity or as a softened version of assimilationist demands.
explanations.D: This choice attributes a stronger, more categorical judgment to the author than the text supports. The passage does not claim that teaching any prestige standard necessarily disrespects students' existing dialects; it presents this as one possible, contested reading of the compromise rather than as the author's own settled conclusion.

questions.stem: The author's claim that certain choices “relocate, rather than eliminate, the normative judgment” in the fifth paragraph most nearly means that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: descriptive linguists have moved their research focus away from grammar toward vocabulary in recent decades.

choices.label: B
choices.text: normative judgments about language have become less common overall as descriptive linguistics has grown more influential.

choices.label: C
choices.text: prescriptivist grammarians and descriptivist linguists have gradually reached general agreement on which usages should count as standard.

choices.label: D
choices.text: descriptivist documentation choices still involve judgments about legitimate language, only less explicitly than rule-making.

explanations.A: This choice misreads the sentence as a claim about the direction of linguistic research generally, which the passage does not make. The sentence describes what happens within a single act of dictionary compilation, not a broader shift in the field's research priorities.
explanations.B: This choice contradicts the paragraph's actual claim. Rather than arguing that normative judgments have become less common, the paragraph argues that they persist within descriptivist practice, only in a less explicit, less openly acknowledged form than in prescriptivism.
explanations.C: This choice introduces an agreement between prescriptivist and descriptivist scholars that the passage does not describe. The paragraph's point is about the hidden normativity within descriptivist method itself, not about convergence between the two camps on which usages count as standard.
explanations.D: Correct. The phrase indicates that decisions about which usage to document as representative of a language still constitute normative judgments about what counts as legitimate, even though these judgments are embedded in method rather than announced as explicit rules, unlike prescriptivist grammar.

questions.stem: A team compiling a new dictionary decides to include only usage drawn from published books, national newspapers, and academic journals, excluding transcripts of everyday conversation, regional speech, and social media, on the grounds that these sources best represent “the language as it is actually used.” Based on the passage, this decision would most likely be characterized as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: a straightforward example of prescriptivism, since the dictionary team is actively enforcing rules about correct usage.

choices.label: B
choices.text: descriptivism failing at neutrality, since choosing which sources represent the language favors certain registers.

choices.label: C
choices.text: an unbiased application of descriptivist method, since the team relies exclusively on published, verifiable sources rather than on any individual's personal judgment.

choices.label: D
choices.text: irrelevant to the passage's argument, since dictionary compilation is a practical matter unrelated to the theoretical dispute between prescriptivism and descriptivism.

explanations.A: This choice misapplies prescriptivism to a scenario that does not involve enforcing rules on speakers. The dictionary team is not correcting anyone's usage or declaring forms incorrect; they are selecting which sources to sample, a descriptivist activity the passage specifically argues is not neutral, rather than a prescriptivist one.
explanations.B: Correct. By excluding everyday conversation, regional speech, and social media in favor of published, edited sources, the team has made a normative choice about which usage counts as representative of “the language,” precisely the kind of hidden gatekeeping decision the fifth paragraph argues descriptivism cannot fully avoid.
explanations.C: This choice treats reliance on published sources as inherently unbiased, which the passage directly contradicts. The fifth paragraph specifically argues that choices about which sources to sample, even verifiable, published ones, embed normative judgments about legitimacy rather than escaping them.
explanations.D: This choice dismisses the scenario as unrelated to the passage's theoretical concerns, but dictionary compilation practices are exactly what the fifth paragraph uses to illustrate its central claim about descriptivism's hidden normativity. The scenario is a direct application of that argument, not an unrelated practical matter.

questions.stem: Which of the following findings would most strengthen the passage's claim that unregulated linguistic variation carries genuine practical costs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A study finding that most speakers can easily understand dialects other than their own after a brief period of exposure.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A study finding that regional dialects have historically incorporated vocabulary from neighboring languages far more often than prestige standards have.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A study finding applicants using nonstandard grammar, with identical qualifications, significantly less likely to receive offers.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A study finding that written and spoken registers of the same language typically differ more than two spoken registers differ from each other.

explanations.A: This choice would tend to undercut, rather than strengthen, the claim that variation carries genuine costs, since easy mutual intelligibility across dialects after brief exposure would suggest that unregulated variation causes fewer communication problems than the passage suggests.
explanations.B: This choice concerns the historical sources of vocabulary in different dialects, which does not address whether unregulated variation currently imposes any practical costs on speakers navigating shared institutions. It is not relevant to the claim being tested.
explanations.C: Correct. The passage claims that command of a prestige register functions as a gatekeeping credential for access to social and economic institutions. Direct evidence that nonstandard grammatical usage reduced job offers despite identical qualifications and content would provide concrete support for exactly this claim about the real, practical costs of linguistic variation.
explanations.D: This choice concerns differences between written and spoken registers generally, which does not address the specific claim about practical costs imposed on speakers of nonstandard dialects navigating institutions that privilege a prestige standard. It does not bear on the claim in question.

questions.stem: Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the passage's claim that even descriptive dictionaries cannot achieve full neutrality in documenting language?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Evidence that most descriptive dictionaries are produced by teams of professional linguists rather than by individual authors.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Evidence that descriptive dictionaries are generally longer, in total number of documented entries, than prescriptive dictionaries covering the same language.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Evidence that public trust in dictionaries as authoritative sources has declined somewhat in the past several decades.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Evidence of a descriptive dictionary sampling usage in exact proportion to actual frequency across every register and group, with nothing over- or under-represented.

explanations.A: This choice concerns who compiles a dictionary, teams versus individuals, which has no bearing on whether the resulting sample of documented usage is representative or systematically skewed. Team composition does not address the neutrality of the underlying sampling method.
explanations.B: This choice concerns the total number of entries in a dictionary, which does not address whether the process of selecting which usage to include was neutral or involved hidden normative choices. Length is not evidence of representativeness.
explanations.C: This choice concerns public trust in dictionaries, a matter of perception rather than of whether a dictionary's sampling method actually achieves neutrality. Declining trust does not show, one way or the other, whether a specific compilation process was representative.
explanations.D: Correct. The passage's claim depends on the premise that selecting which usage to document inevitably involves non-neutral choices favoring some registers over others. Direct evidence that a specific project achieved genuinely proportional representation across every register, dialect, and social group, with no systematic over- or under-representation, would show that full neutrality is achievable in practice, directly undermining the passage's claim.
